title: Modern Cedar Outdoor Storage Bench
summary: A deck box that reads as modern furniture: cedar picket paneling over two 2x4 frames, a slat floor on cleats, and a hinged lid that seats two.
description:
Every deck accumulates the same homeless objects — cushions, hose nozzles, citronella, the cover for the grill — and this bench swallows all of it while giving two people somewhere to sit. Seven cedar fence pickets do the entire visible surface; the structure is just two 2x4 side frames the paneling hangs on.

The design's quiet detail is in step 2: the first picket is held UP 3/4" from the frame bottom, which means the paneling — not the frame — meets the deck. The 2x4s stay off the wet surface, and the box appears to float a hair off the ground. Small move, does two jobs; keep it consistent all the way around.

The lid is its own assembly built in place but never attached to the box: lid supports laid loose on the frame tops, planks screwed to the supports, then hinges at the back. The 'cut out the front access' instruction is the finger pull — a notch jigsawed in the front lid edge, which costs nothing and spares you hardware. One honest caveat: picket boxes are weather-resistant, not waterproof. Store cushions in a bin inside it, or accept that a driving rain finds the gaps.

tools.note: The finger-pull notch in the front lid edge.
Rough pickets, and a lid people sit on — sand the top thoroughly.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
materials.note: All the paneling, floor, and lid. 1x6 boards substitute directly for a smoother look.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Floor cleats and short floor slats.

materials.name: Exterior hinges
materials.note: Small hinges at the back of the lid.

materials.name: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2", self-tapping, star drive
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive
materials.note: All the paneling. Staples or brads in matching lengths substitute.

cutList.note: Measure and cut to fit your box.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut six frame boards at 15-3/4" from the 2x4. Cut nine front and back paneling at 35-3/4" and ten end paneling and floor slats at 15-3/4" from the Cedar fence picket. Cut two floor cleats at 34-3/4" and two short floor slats at 12-3/4" from the 1x2. Cut two lid supports at 15-1/2" from the 1x3. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to one 8 ft 2x4, one 8 ft 1x2 and one 8 ft 1x3. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.
materials: 2x4, 8 ft
Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
1x2, 8 ft
1x3, 8 ft

steps.title: Build the two side frames
steps.body: Screw three 2x4s into a U per side with the 2-1/2" screws, two per joint. These two frames are the entire structure — everything else is skin.
materials: 2x4, 8 ft
Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Panel the ends — held up 3/4"
steps.body: Attach the end pickets to the frames with the 1-1/4" screws, two per end, holding the FIRST picket 3/4" up from the frame bottom. That reveal puts cedar, not framing lumber, at deck level, and floats the box visually. Keep it consistent — it's the kind of detail that's invisible done right and glaring done unevenly.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Panel the front and back
steps.body: Attach the long pickets to the frames, matching the end paneling's heights so the courses wrap the corners level.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Add the floor cleats
steps.body: Screw the 1x2 cleats to the inside faces with the shorter screws — measure your actual box for length. They carry the floor, and everything stored in the bench.
materials: 1x2, 8 ft
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Lay the floor
steps.body: Set the floor slats on the cleats and fasten with 1-1/4" screws, nails, or staples. Leave small gaps — anything that drips in needs a way out the bottom.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
1x2, 8 ft
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Stage the lid supports — do not attach them
steps.body: Lay the two 1x3 supports across the frame tops, held to the back, small gap at the front. They LOOK like box parts; they are lid parts. Screwing them to the box here is this build's one classic mistake — the lid would never open.
materials: 1x3, 8 ft

steps.title: Build the lid in place, hinge, and notch
steps.body:
Lay the lid pickets over the supports and screw pickets to SUPPORTS ONLY — the lid becomes one panel you can lift straight off. Hinge it to the box at the back with the exterior hinges.

Jigsaw a shallow finger notch in the front lid edge for lifting. Sand the whole top well (it's seating), and either let the cedar silver or oil it annually. Cushions live longer in a bin inside — a picket box sheds weather, it doesn't seal it out.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
1x3, 8 ft
Exterior hinges
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

images.alt: Modern cedar deck storage bench with hinged slat lid
